narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 52.6% chance that you would not pass—crossing the threshold we use to make admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 and LSAT score of 29, alongside your Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles of 3 and 2 respectively, your lower-middle family income bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. Compared to students who typically pass the bar, where successful applicants average much higher deciles around 6, an LSAT near 37, and a GPA of 3.24, your profile showed some gaps that the model weighed heavily. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, as being in the lower part of your class at that stage strongly signaled potential challenges ahead, unlike the higher rankings common among those who pass. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile, which also pulled the prediction toward failure by reflecting early academic struggles relative to peers who succeed. On a more positive note, your family income bracket actually worked in your favor, nudging the model toward expecting success since lower-middle backgrounds are less common but not barriers for many who pass—about 9% of them share that circumstance. While your full-time enrollment aligns with the vast majority of passers (over 93%), it wasn't a top driver here. Overall, these academic deciles overshadowed the strengths in your background, leading to the rejection. This isn't a judgment on your potential or determination—many students in similar positions have improved dramatically with targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37, strengthening your undergrad record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and seeking mentorship from law school alumni who started from lower deciles. Building a narrative of upward trajectory in your next application, perhaps with letters highlighting your resilience as a full-time aspiring lawyer from a modest background, could shift the model's view. You're capable of passing the bar with the right preparation, and reapplying after addressing these key areas gives you a strong shot at admission next cycle. Keep pushing—you've got this.
